answer:
**Iqbal's "Goristan-e-Shahi" ("The Royal Graveyard")** evokes the tombs of Mughal emperors in Lahore's royal cemetery as symbols of vanished imperial splendor, much like **the anonymous speaker of "The Wanderer"** (from the *Old English Elegies*) surveys the frost-covered ruins of ancient halls, once filled with mead-hall feasts and warrior joy, now empty under wyrd (fate).[^1][^2]

### Most Reverent Lines
- **Iqbal**: "A graveyard, yes, but dust of **high degree**, / A fallen nation’s **final treasury**." Here, the graves command awe as noble remnants, worthy of tears.[^3]
- **Wanderer**: "Well is it for him who seeks **grace**, / comfort from the **Father in heaven**, where for us all **stability** stands." Reverence turns to divine consolation amid earthly ruin.[^4]

### Most Disenchanted Lines
- **Iqbal**: "**Is this** the end of all their **mighty fame**, / Whose statecraft put **Decline** itself to shame?" A stark query on glory's futility.[^5]
- **Wanderer**: "'Where has the **horse** gone? Where the **warrior**? Where the **treasure-giver**? / Where the **feast-benches**? Where the **hall-joys**? / Alas bright cup! Alas mailed warrior! / Alas prince’s pride! How that time **passed**, / dark under night’s helm, **as if it never were**!'" Pure lament for obliterated life.[^6]

The **Wanderer** clings **closer to the past**, his speech drenched in intimate grief—hall-sad exile, dreams of embracing his dead "gold-friend," kinsmen fading like sea-birds—binding sorrow in his "spirit-lock" without release.[^7] **Iqbal**, by contrast, is **more at peace with the loss**, viewing the cemetery philosophically as part of time's cycle ("The world is used to seeing nations fall"), even hinting at renewal ("This cloud can make the desert dust a rose").[^8] Where the Wanderer aches personally, Iqbal mourns a collective history with detached wonder, already seeding future dawns.

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 9 (*Goristan-e-Shahi*), [para.](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-2e4cf2).

[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 9, [para.](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-49c46c).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).

[^5]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 9, [para.](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-48606f).

[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11, [para.](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^8]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 9, [paras.](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-d9fecb) (p-d9fecb, p-9407fa).
